title: Equilibrium Bitcoin Pricing
abstract: We offer a general equilibrium analysis of cryptocurrency pricing. The fundamental value of the cryptocurrency is its stream of net transactional benefits, which depend on its future prices. This implies that, in addition to fundamentals, equilibrium prices reflect sunspots. This in turn implies multiple equilibria and extrinsic volatility, that is, cryptocurrency prices fluctuate even when fundamentals are constant. To match our model to the data, we construct indices measuring the net transactional benefits of Bitcoin. In our calibration, part of the variations in Bitcoin returns reflects changes in net transactional benefits, but a larger share reflects extrinsic volatility.

article.introduction:
What is the fundamental valueof cryptocurrencies? Do high market valuations reflect fundamentals or speculation? Does high volatility reflect investors' irrationality? We offer a framework to address these questions. In our framework, the fundamentals of a cryptocurrency are the net transactional benefits it is expected to provide.1Yet in addition to changes in fundamentals, variation in equilibrium cryptocurrency prices reflects sunspot-driven extrinsic volatility. The contribution of this paper is to theoretically analyze and empirically quantify the relationship between cryptocurrency prices, fundamentals, and extrinsic volatility.
  Our theoretical model involves overlapping generations of agents with stochastic endowments who can trade standard fiat money (such as the dollar) and a cryptocurrency (such as Bitcoin). Although both fiat money and cryptocurrency can be used to purchase consumption goods, the cryptocurrency can provide transactional benefits that standard money cannot. For example, citizens of Venezuela or Zimbabwe can use Bitcoin to conduct transactions even when their national currencies and banking systems are in disarray. Cryptocurrencies can be used for cross-border transfers when high costs or government controls hinder transfers via traditional financial institutions. However, in addition to providing these transactional benefits, cryptocurrencies come with costs: limited convertibility into traditional currencies, transaction costs on exchanges, fees that agents must pay to have their transactions mined, and the risk of a crash of the cryptocurrency.
  In our model, investors rationally choose their demand for cryptocurrency based on their beliefs about future prices and net transactional benefits. This yields a Euler equation relating the current price of the cryptocurrency to the expectation of the stochastic discount factor multiplied by the sum of the future price and net transactional benefits. In this sense, transactional benefits are to cryptocurrencies what dividends are to stocks. There is however a major difference. In perfect markets, dividends do not depend on stock prices and provide a real anchor for valuation. In contrast, the transactional benefits provided by a cryptocurrency depend on its price: the higher the price of the cryptocurrency, the stronger is its purchasing power relative to the standard currency, and consequently the higher are the transactional benefits it delivers. Because cryptocurrency prices reflect beliefs about future prices rather than real variables independent of prices, equilibrium can reflect exogenous sunspots.2This difference between cryptocurrencies and stocks precludes a standard Campbell and Shiller (1988b) decomposition. In this paper, we propose an alternative decomposition.
  Characterizing equilibrium currency price processes is challenging. One of our contributions is to offer a new characterization of two classes of equilibria. First, we consider “constant-price equilibria” in which, each period, a sunspot can lead to a crash whereby the cryptocurrency price permanently drops to zero while the standard currency price remains positive. In this type of equilibrium, prices of both currencies are constant before and after the crash. We show that there are multiple constant-price equilibria, one for each possible constant value of the crash probability. Second, we consider “volatile-price equilibria” in which sunspots can trigger price changes each period. To characterize these equilibria, we consider an arbitrary horizon ofNperiods. From periodNonward, the continuation equilibrium falls in the class of constant-price equilibria. There are multiple continuation constant-price equilibria, one for each value of the crash probability prevailing from periodNonward. These different crash probabilities determine the state of the sunspot at that time as well as the corresponding equilibrium price.3By backward induction, we then obtain the equilibrium cryptocurrency price at periodN−1$N-1$as a function of the state of the sunspot atN−1$N-1$, the transition probabilities from that state to the period-Nstate, and the corresponding possible values of the cryptocurrency price in periodN. Iterating backward yields the sequence of equilibrium cryptocurrency prices in all periods beforeN−1$N-1$.
  There are multiple equilibria, one for each possibledistributionof the sample path of the sunspot. Within each equilibrium there is extrinsic volatility, since cryptocurrency prices change in response torealizationsof the sunspot variable, even when fundamental variables remain constant. Although related, equilibrium multiplicity and extrinsic volatility are different concepts. On the one hand, there are multiple constant-price equilibria in which, except at the time of the crash, there is no volatility. On the other hand, within a given volatile-price equilibrium there is volatility, as the sunspot and hence the cryptocurrency prices vary randomlyon the equilibrium path.4
  For isoelastic utilities, we show that the price of the cryptocurrency increases with its transactional benefits and decreases with its crash risk. Moreover, risk-averse investors require a risk premium in compensation for crash risk.
  To confront our theory to the data, we construct time series of Bitcoin prices and proxies for transactional costs and benefits of Bitcoin. First, we collect the transaction fees paid by Bitcoin users to miners. These fees are high when trading is large, leading to congestion in the blockchain. Thus, high transaction fees are not only costly in and of themselves but also signal costs associated with congestion. Second, we browse the web archives to collect information on events likely to affect the costs and benefits of transacting in Bitcoin. We categorize these events into two groups. The first group contains events indicative of the ease with which Bitcoin can be exchanged against other currencies, such as a new currency becoming tradable against Bitcoin or the shutdown of a large platform. The second group contains events affecting the ease with which Bitcoin can be used to purchase goods and services, such as merchants starting to accept Bitcoin. Based on these two sets of events, we construct two indices that proxy for transactional costs and transactional benefits. Third, we collect data about Bitcoin thefts and hacks. Using these data, we calibrate our model. For simplicity, in the calibration we assume risk-neutral investors. Empirically, risk neutrality is likely to be an admissible shortcut because Liu and Tsyvinski (2021) find that, for our sample period, cryptocurrency returns are not significantly correlated with consumption or production growth. In the risk-neutral case, we obtain an expression for cryptocurrency required returns that holds across all possible equilibria.5
  We specify transactional costs and benefits as linear functions of the above-described proxies. The calibrated coefficients imply required returns that increase in transactional costs and decrease in transactional benefits. That said, we do not claim statistical significance or estimation of population parameters because of the relatively short size of our sample and the likely non stationarity of our variables. Calibrated required Bitcoin weekly returns start very high (above 10% per week), but then decline, to 2% at the end of the sample period. Crash risk accounts for around 11 percentage points of the required return at the beginning of the sample period. Its contribution to required returns then decreases to close to zero. The costs associated with mining fees and congestion are negligible except for 2017. The difficulty in exchanging Bitcoin against standard currency adds almost 10 percentage points to the required return at the beginning of the sample. Within a year, however, its contribution drops to around 8 percentage points and then remains around that level. The contribution of hack risk is small. Against these costs, the calibrated transactional benefit component starts around zero but increases until 2015. From that point on, it hovers around 8%. This is admittedly high and maybe implausible. It is useful, however, to compare this magnitude to that of the cost of cross-border fund transfers, which cryptocurrencies can help avoid. Data from the World Bank suggest that remittance costs are around 6%.6
  Although our calibration quantifies the effect of fundamentals on required expected returns, it also shows that changes in fundamentals explain only a small share (around 5%) of the variance of Bitcoin returns. Under the hypothesis that our model is well-specified and our proxies accurate, this implies that most of Bitcoin return variation reflects extrinsic volatility unrelated to fundamentals.
  Literature. Our theoretical analysis builds on an overlapping-generation model to micro-found money (Allais (1947), Samuelson (1958), Wallace (1980), Tirole (1985)). This type of model has been extended to cryptocurrencies by Saleh (2020) and Garratt and Wallace (2018). In a one-currency model, Saleh (2020) compares equilibrium prices and welfare in two protocols, namely, proof-of-burn and proof-of-work. Garratt and Wallace (2018) use the Kareken and Wallace (1981) model of several currencies to analyze the joint determination of the prices of a cryptocurrency and a standard currency. In their analysis, in each period there can be a sunspot leading to a crash. This gives rise to the constant-price equilibria we also analyze. The incremental contribution of our theoretical analysis, relative to Garratt and Wallace (2018) is twofold.7First, for constant-price equilibria we solve explicitly for the cryptocurrency price in the isoelastic case. This enables us to conduct comparative statics analysis of cryptocurrency pricing. Second, in addition to constant-price equilibria, we characterize a new class of equilibria, volatile-price equilibria, in which sunspots trigger continuous price changes even when fundamentals remain constant.8
  Schilling and Uhlig (2019) study the interaction between Bitcoin and dollar in a model in which agents live forever but alternate between consumption and production, which generates nondouble coincidence of wants. Schilling and Uhlig (2019) show that if consumption and the price of dollar are independent from the price of Bitcoin, then the latter follows a martingale. This is similar to the condition we obtain in the risk-neutral case, except that the pricing equation in Schilling and Uhlig (2019) does not feature the transactional costs and benefits of the cryptocurrency, which play a central role in our analysis.
  Also related to the monetary theory literature, Chiu and Koeppl (2019), Zhu and Hendry (2019), Fernández-Villaverde and Sanches (2019), Pagnotta (2021), and Auer, Monnet and Shin (2021) extend the Lagos and Wright (2005) model to the case of cryptocurrencies. An important feature of the models studied by Chiu and Koeppl (2019) and Pagnotta (2021) is the risk of an attack on the network, which decreases with the hashpower that miners dedicate to the network. In Pagnotta (2021), if the cryptocurrency is expected to be safe, its price is high. This induces many agents to engage in mining,9thus making the cryptocurrency safe. But there is also a rational expectations equilibrium in which the cryptocurrency is expected to be risky, its price is low, and there is little mining. The multiplicity of equilibria in Pagnotta (2021) differs from that arising in our model. In Pagnotta (2021), multiplicity reflects the feedback loop between prices and mining while in our paper it reflects that investors can coordinate on different beliefs about the likelihood of a crash. Fernández-Villaverde and Sanches (2019) and Choi and Rocheteau (2021) analyze models in which agents can create private monies at a cost and show there exist equilibria in which the value of private monies eventually vanishes. The focus on deterministic perfect foresight equilibria in Choi and Rocheteau (2021) contrasts with our focus on stochastic equilibria. The focus of Fernández-Villaverde and Sanches (2019) on the consequences of the shape of entrepreneurs' cost function and the quantity of money differs from our focus on the transactional benefits and costs of the cryptocurrency and on the role of agents' beliefs for the risk of a crash. Auer, Monnet and Shin (2021) jointly analyze the use of cryptocurrencies in a Lagos-Wright framework and the strategic contribution of validators to the blockchain. Their emphasis on the optimal design of the validation mechanism differs from our emphasis on the dynamics of cryptocurrency prices in general equilibrium.
  Another strand of the literature to which our paper is related focuses on platforms within which agents use cryptocurrencies to reap gains from trade. In Athey et al. (2016), the platform is used to transfer funds abroad. An important ingredient is the risk of a fatal flaw making the platform vulnerable to an attack. This is related to the risk of a cryptocurrency crash in our model, but our analysis encompasses the case in which a crash is triggered by a real event as in Athey et al. (2016), and the case in which it is triggered by a sunspot. Cong, Li and Wang (2021) also consider a platform in which agents use tokens to conduct peer to peer transactions, and thus benefit from transactional benefits. An important ingredient in Cong, Li and Wang (2021) is network externalities: the larger the number of users, the larger the transactional benefits each user enjoys. This implies that there can be equilibrium multiplicity. But the source of multiplicity in Cong, Li and Wang (2021), network externalities, differs from that in our paper, sunspots. In Cong, Li and Wang (2021), transactional benefits increase with platform productivity. Network externalities amplify the impact of productivity shocks on token prices. The corresponding “excess volatility” of cryptocurrency prices differs from the extrinsic volatility in our analysis: in Cong, Li and Wang (2021) excess volatility reflects changes in fundamentals, whereas in our analysis extrinsic volatility is unrelated to fundamentals. Sockin and Xiong (2020) also offer a model of cryptocurrency valuation in the presence of positive network externalities in which, by delegating control to users, tokenization creates commitment not to exploit users.
  On the empirical side, Makarov and Schoar (2020), Borri and Shakhnov (2022), and Hautsch, Scheuch, and Voigt (2018) document mispricings and arbitrage opportunities for Bitcoin. Rather than focus on arbitrage, we focus on equilibrium cryptocurrency prices. Our paper thus relates to Liu and Tsyvinski (2021), Liu, Tsyvinski and Wu (2022), Bianchi (2020), and Bhambhwani, Delikouras and Korniotis (2019). Liu and Tsyvinski (2021) document that cryptocurrency returns are not significantly correlated with consumption or production growth, but are exposed to cryptocurrency network factors. Liu, Tsyvinski and Wu (2022) show that cryptocurrency market, size, and momentum, capture the cross-section of cryptocurrencies' expected returns. In contrast our empirical focus is on measuring the costs and benefits of Bitcoin and using these measures to calibrate our theoretical model. Our indices measuring the ease and cost of using Bitcoin are in the same line as the index constructed by Auer and Claessens (2018) to capture the impact of regulation on cryptocurrencies. Differences between Auer and Claessens (2018) and our paper include their focus on regulatory events and our reliance on a theoretical model.
  The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIpresents the model. In SectionII, we characterize equilibria in the risk-averse and risk-neutral cases. SectionIIIdescribes the data used in the calibration. Results are discussed in SectionIV. SectionVconcludes. All proofs are in theAppendix. AnInternet Appendixdetails all events used to construct our indices.10
article.I. Model:
Time is discrete and divided into periods and the horizon is infinite. There is one consumption good and three assets: a cryptocurrency (e.g., Bitcoin) in supplyXt$X_{t}$in periodt, a standard currency (e.g., dollar) in fixed supplym, and a risk-free asset in zero net supply.
  There are investors, miners, and hackers. All are competitive and take prices as given. We consider miners and hackers in order to introduce two important features of the cryptocurrency, namely, the creation of new coins and the risk of hacks. In our model the actions of miners and hackers are very simple: they perform their activity and then sell their cryptocurrency holdings and consume. In contrast, we analyze in detail investors' consumption and savings decisions which, combined with market clearing, pin down equilibrium pricing.
  In each periodt, a new generation of miners is born. Miners born in periodtmine untilt+1$t+1$, at which point they get rewarded by newly created coins,Xt+1−Xt$X_{t+1}-X_{t}$, and transaction fees. In periodt+1$t+1$, miners sell their coins against consumption goods, which they consume (along with the fees they received) before exiting the market. Denote byct+1m$c_{t+1}^{m}$the consumption of miners in periodt+1$t+1$.
  Similarly, in each periodta new generation of hackers is born. They try to steal some cryptocurrency, for example, by hacking a cryptocurrency exchange (like Bitfinex in 2016) or a decentralized autonomous organization (like the DAO built on top of Ethereum, also in 2016).11The fraction they manage to steal is a random variable in [0,1], which we denote byht+1$h_{t+1}$. The indext+1$t+1$reflects the fact that the fraction stolen is not known by investors int, but is discovered int+1$t+1$only. In periodt+1$t+1$, hackers sell their stolen coins against consumption goods, which they consume before exiting the market. Their consumption is denoted byct+1h$c_{t+1}^{h}$.
  Finally, a mass-one continuum of investors are born in each period. They live and consume during two periods, have separable additive utilityu(.), withu′>0$u^{^{\prime }}>0$andu′′≤0$u^{^{\prime \prime }}\le 0$, and discount factor β. In each period, their utility is defined over positive consumption and the transactional benefits of using cryptocurrency. To simplify the analysis, we assume that consumption and transactional benefits enter additively in the utility function. To initialize the model, at date 1 there is also a generation of old investors, miners, and hackers, who hold the supply of cryptocurrencyX1and standard currencym.
  In periodt, each young investor is endowed withet$e_{t}$units of consumption good, can buyqt$q_{t}$units of cryptocurrency—or coins—at unit pricept$p_{t}$, andq̂t$\hat{q}_{t}$units of standard currency at unit pricep̂t$\hat{p }_{t}$, and can savest$s_{t}$. For notational simplicity, the consumption good is the numeraire. Thus,pt$p_{t}$(p̂t$\hat{p}_{t}$) is the number of units of consumption good that can be purchased with one unit of cryptocurrency (standard currency) in periodt.
  We assume thatθt+1≥−1$\theta _{t+1}\ge -1$.13Equation (2) then implies that old agents sell all of their holdings of cryptocurrency(1−ht+1)qt$(1-h_{t+1})q_{t}$to increase their consumptionĉt+1o$\hat{c}^o_{t+1}$. Note that in our theoretical and empirical analyses, we assume that{Xt}t>0$\lbrace X_{t}\rbrace _{t>0}$,{θt}t>0$\lbrace \theta _{t}\rbrace _{t>0}$and{φt}t>0$\lbrace \varphi _{t}\rbrace _{t>0}$are exogenous processes, independent of the actions of the agents in the market.
  As in Garratt and Wallace (2018), we allow for the possibility that, at the end of each periodtwith probabilityπt$\pi _{t}$there is a crash, and the cryptocurrency price permanently drops to zero, that is,pt+s=0$p_{t+s}=0$, for alls>0$s>0$. As Garratt and Wallace (2018) discuss, the occurrence of a crash can be due to a sunspot (see Cass and Shell (1983)). Under that interpretation, the crash is a purely extrinsic random variable—it does not reflect any change in the fundamentals but rather an extrinsic change in beliefs, triggered by a sunspot, independent of other variables (e.g.,θt$\theta _{t}$,φt$ \varphi _{t}$, orpt$p_{t}$).
  A crash occurs if all agents believe that the cryptocurrency is worthless. In that case, they reject payment in the cryptocurrency, which becomes worthless, and the agents' belief is self-fulfilling. An alternative interpretation of the crash is that it is triggered by a real event, such as the discovery of a flaw in the protocol, a successful attack on the blockchain (for example, a 51% attack), or a sudden change in the political and legal environment making it impossible to use the cryptocurrency.
  In our model, the standard currency price could also go to zero. As Starr (1974) shows, however, this can be prevented when the government levies taxes that must be paid in that currency: even if agents anticipate that others will not to accept the standard currency, as long as they know that the government will accept it for the payment of taxes, demand for the standard currency is strictly positive at prices bounded away from zero.14Accordingly, in our analyzes, we focus on equilibria in which the price of the standard currency is strictly positive at all times.
  The sequence of events in each period is summarized in Figure1. Under the sunspot interpretation of a crash, whether a crash occurs in periodtis determined by the realization of a random variable at the end of periodtindependent of all the other variables in agents' information set in periodtand with a distribution governed by the probabilityπt$\pi _{t}$of a crash. Furthermore, we allowπt$\pi _{t}$to be itself a random variable determined at the beginning of periodt. That is, the probability of a crash can be time-varying and stochastic.
article.II. Equilibria:
Proposition 1.The equilibrium price of the cryptocurrency in periodtis such that
  
  In theAppendix, we complete the proof of Proposition1by showing that (11) also holds when the constraint that consumption is positive binds.
  Fundamental value, price, and transactional benefit. Equation (11) states that the price of the cryptocurrency in periodtis equal to the present value of the expectation of the product of three terms. The first term is the pricing kernel that captures the correlation between the marginal utility of consumption and the cryptocurrency price. The second term reflects the risk of hacks. The third term is the sum of the price of the cryptocurrency in periodt+1$t+1$and its net transactional benefit.
  Equation (12) rewrites (11) to show that the equilibrium pricept$p_{t}$is the sum of the discounted expected transactional benefits brought by the cryptocurrencyTt+kpt+k$\mathcal {T} _{t+k}p_{t+k}$, which corresponds to its fundamental value. Equation (12) is similar to its counterpart for stocks, except that instead of stemming from the transactional benefitsTt+kpt+k$\mathcal {T}_{t+k}p_{t+k}$, the fundamental value stems from the firm's dividend. This points to an essential difference between the fundamental value of a currency and that of a stock. For stocks, in perfect markets, current valuation reflects the expectation of future dividends, which do not depend on future stock prices, and thus valuation is anchored by a fundamental variable independent of future prices. In contrast, for currencies, there is no such anchor as endogenous future prices determine transactional benefits. This lack of exogenous anchor raises the possibility of equilibrium multiplicity and extrinsic volatility, as we explain below.16Equation (12) further shows that, for the cryptocurrency price to be strictly positive at timet, its net transactional benefitTt+k$\mathcal {T}_{t+k}$must be positive for somek. However, equation (12) does not rule out the possibility that net transactional benefits could be negative in the short term—they would be simply need to become sufficiently positive in the long term in expectation.
  In our stochastic infinite horizon setting, it is difficult to explicitly solve for equilibrium prices when agents are risk-averse. There is one relatively simple case, however, in which we can further characterize equilibrium prices: equilibria in which prices are constant until there is a crash, bringing the cryptocurrency price down to zero. In this subsection, the crash probability is constant and denoted by π.17By a slight abuse of language, we refer to these equilibria as constant price equilibria, although at the time of the crash they involve a sharp change in prices. Admittedly, these constant-price equilibria are not very plausible because they do not allow for volatility except at the time of the crash. That said, they offer a simple laboratory in which to develop intuition about the economics of currency pricing. They also serve as a building block, which we use below to characterize more general and plausible equilibria with volatile prices.
  To study constant-price equilibria, for simplicity we assume that endowments, cryptocurrency supply, costs, and benefits are constant, that is,et=e$ e_{t}=e$,Xt=X$X_{t}=X$,φt=φ$\varphi _{t}=\varphi$,θt=θ$\theta _{t}=\theta$andht=h$ h_t=h$. In the event of a crash, the cryptocurrency price goes to zero. We also assume(1−h)(1+θ)>1+φ$(1-h)(1+\theta)>1+\varphi$, so that the net transactional benefit of the cryptocurrency is positive, which is necessary for the cryptocurrency to have a positive price. Finally, we assume thatu′′<0$ u^{^{\prime \prime }}<0$, so we can analyze the consequences of investors' risk aversion for currency pricing.
  Withu′′<0$u^{^{\prime \prime }}<0$, there exists a unique solution to equation (18).
  We thus obtain our second proposition:
  Proposition 2.There exists a constant-price equilibrium if and only if there exists a solution(p,p̂)$(p, \hat{p})$to the system of equations
  
  
  Equation (19) stems from (17), the equilibrium condition for the standard currency, while (20) stems from (14), the equilibrium condition for the cryptocurrency. For general preferences, it is not easy to prove existence of a solution to this system of equations and to characterize equilibria. As we show below, however, for isoelastic utility functions the problem is more tractable.
  For tractability, we assume isoelastic utility, with constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) coefficient denoted by γ. Forγ≠1$\gamma \ne 1$,u(c)=c1−γ−11−γ$u(c)= \frac{c^{1-\gamma }-1}{1-\gamma }$, while forγ=1$\gamma =1$,u(c)=ln(c)$u(c)=\ln (c)$. Also, to avoid heavy notation but without qualitatively affecting the results, we assumeh=0$h=0$andφ=0$\varphi =0$and we denoteD≡β(1−π)(1+θ)$D\equiv \beta (1-\pi )(1+\theta )$. The variableDcan be interpreted as a generalized discount factor for the cryptocurrency. We thus obtain the following proposition.
  Proposition 3.If investors have power utility, then for any crash probabilityπ∈(0,1)$\pi \in (0,1)$and benefit θ such that
  
  
  The equilibrium conditions (22) and (23) give the capitalizations of the cryptocurrency and of the standard currency,Xp$Xp$andmp̂$m\hat{p}$, respectively, as a function of the parameters θ, π, and γ. The equilibrium existence condition (21) defines an upper bound on the crash probability π as stated in the next corollary.
  Corollary 1.Condition (21) is equivalent to
  
  
  For each value of the crash probabilityπ<π¯$\pi <\overline{\pi }$, there exists a pair of strictly positive prices such that (22) and (23) hold, that is, there exists a constant-price equilibrium. As π varies, there is a continuum of constant-price equilibria. If one interprets crashes in terms of sunspots, different values of π correspond to different beliefs on which investors coordinate. Since there is a continuum of different possible beliefs π, there is a multiplicity of sunspot equilibria.
  In any constant-price equilibrium, gross of transactional benefits the expected return on the cryptocurrency is negative, since with probability π there is a crash and the return is negative, while with the complementary probability there is no crash and the return is zero. Yet, as stated in the next corollary, the expected return on the cryptocurrency inclusive of the transactional benefit is higher than the equilibrium risk-free rate.
  Corollary 2.In the equilibrium defined in Proposition3, the cryptocurrency commands a strictly positive risk premium, that is,
  
  
  The magnitude of the cryptocurrency risk premium is obviously related to the crash probability π. Note that not only the return of cryptocurrency but also the risk-free raterdepend on π (equation (16)), so that the variation of this risk premium with respect to π is not a priori straightforward. When the crash probability tends to the upper boundπ¯$\overline{\pi }$defined in Corollary1, the cryptocurrency price tends to zero, and thus its market capitalizationXp$Xp$becomes small relative to the market capitalization of the standard currencymp̂$m\hat{p}$. Old investors' consumption is then less affected by the occurrence of a crash, and the cryptocurrency risk premium tends to zero. This suggests that the risk-neutral case we study below is a good approximation of our general model when the capitalization of the cryptocurrency is low relative to that of the standard currency.19
  For the logarithmic utility case (γ=1$\gamma =1$), the equilibrium conditions (21), (22), and (23) simplify and yield an explicit solution for the equilibrium price vector, as stated in the next proposition.
  Proposition 4.If the investors have log utility and
  
  
  Consistent with intuition, equation (29) implies that the cryptocurrency price is decreasing in crash risk (π) and increasing in transactional benefits (θ). In contrast, the standard currency price is increasing in the probability of a crash, and decreasing in transactional benefits. The larger the probability π, the more likely it is that the cryptocurrency will crash, resulting in an increase in the standard currency price. Also, the lower the benefit θ the less the cryptocurrency can compete with the standard currency and the larger the price of the latter.
  To illustrate our analysis, we solve numerically for the cryptocurrency and standard currency prices forβ=1$\beta =1$,γ=.5$\gamma =.5$, 1 and 3, with π ranging between 3% and 7% and θ ranging between 0% and 10%. Figure2plots the capitalization of the cryptocurrencyXp$Xp$as a fraction of total endowmente. As θ increases, the capitalization of the cryptocurrency increases, reflecting that the demand for that currency increases with the transactional benefits it delivers. As π increases, the capitalization of the cryptocurrency decreases because the demand for that currency decreases as its crash risk increases. As the investors' risk aversion increases, the capitalization of the cryptocurrency decreases because investors demand a larger risk premium to bear crash risk.
  The curve depicting the capitalization of the cryptocurrency starts only for values of θ that are above π. This reflects the condition for existence of an equilibrium with strictly positive cryptocurrency price, (21). When θ is too low relative to π, transactional benefits do not make up for crash risk, so that investors are not willing to buy the cryptocurrency at any positive price.
  In Figure3, the capitalization of the cryptocurrency ranges between zero and 35% of the endowment. Current world GDP is around 84.5 trillion dollars, while the current capitalization of Bitcoin is around 1.1 trillion dollars. So, Bitcoin capitalization is currently around 1.3% of world GDP. When crash risk is low (π=3%$\pi =3\%$) and risk aversion is 0.5, one obtains this order of magnitude when θ is lower than 4%. When crash risk is larger (π=7%$\pi =7\%$), one obtains this order of magnitude for θ as large as 8%. Of course, this back of the envelope calculation is to be taken with a generous dose of salt. constant-price equilibria are an extremely simplified representation of reality, in particular because volatility in these equilibria is small relative to its empirical counterpart. Notwithstanding, the analysis of constant-price equilibria paves the way for the more realistic analysis of the next subsection, in which equilibrium prices change in each period and volatility can be large.
  Building on the analysis above, we now construct equilibria in which prices can vary in each period. First, with CRRA, we consider the case in which the only source of variation is sunspots, so that volatility is purely extrinsic. Second, we turn to the case of risk-neutrality, which is tractable enough to yield equilibrium restrictions with both sunspot-driven and fundamental-driven volatility. In both the risk-averse and the risk-neutral cases, there is sunspot-driven equilibrium multiplicity.
  Consider the case in which crashes are arbitrary, up to “sunspot” beliefs. In this context, we now construct an equilibrium in which, in contrast to the previous section in which the probability of a crash is constant, the probability of a crash changes across periods until periodN. From periodNonward, the probability of a crash is constant and the cryptocurrency and standard currency prices are set as in the constant-price equilibrium studied in SectionB.2.
  To capture the dependence of prices to sunspot-driven crash risk, we define the sunspot in periodtasωt=(ξt−1,πt)$\omega _{t}=(\xi _{t-1},\pi _{t})$, whereξt−1=c$\xi _{t-1}=c$in the case of a crash at the end of periodt−1$t-1$andξt−1=nc$\xi _{t-1}=nc$otherwise, whileπt$\pi _{t}$is the probability of a crash during periodt. The sunspotωt$\omega _{t}$is publicly observed at the beginning of periodt. Conditional on(ωt−1,ξt−1)$(\omega _{t-1},\xi _{t-1})$,πt$\pi _{t}$is independent of all other variables in agent's information set in periodt. Moreover, the distribution ofξt$\xi _{t}$depends only onπt$\pi _{t}$. An equilibrium is a mapping fromωt$\omega _{t}$into prices. As before, the price of the standard currency after a crash is given by (24).
  Now turn to the first phase whent≤N−1$t \le N-1$. At the beginning of each periodt≤N−1$t\le N-1$, a sunspot determines current beliefs about the probability of a crash at the end of this period,πt$\pi _{t}$. Specifically, we consider sunspot-driven beliefs such that, at the beginning of periodt≤N−1$ t\le N-1$, with probabilityxt$x_{t}$, the crash probability goes up toπtu≥πt−1$\pi _{t}^{u}\ge \pi _{t-1}$while, with probability1−xt$1-x_{t}$, it decreases toπtd≤πt−1$ \pi _{t}^{d}\le \pi _{t-1}$. Figure3shows the timing of events.
  The left-hand side of (36) is the cryptocurrency price multiplied by the marginal utility of the investor in periodt. The first term on the right-hand side is the probabilityxt+1$x_{t+1}$that crash risk goes up toπt+1u$\pi _{t+1}^{u}$multiplied by the product of the discounted price of the cryptocurrency and the marginal utility of the investor in that state. The second term on the right-hand side is the corresponding term for the state in which the crash risk goes down toπt+1d$\pi _{t+1}^{d}$.
  Similarly for (37), the left-hand side is the standard currency price multiplied by the marginal utility of the investor in periodt. The first two terms on the right-hand side correspond to the cases in which there is no crash in periodtand the crash probability moves up or down at the beginning oft+1$t+1$. The last term on the right-hand side of (37) corresponds to the case in which there is a crash at the end of periodt.
  Equilibrium prices fort≤N−1$t\le N-1$are obtained by backward induction. Att=N−1$ t=N-1$, using (36) and (37) one getsp(πN−1)$p(\pi _{N-1})$andp̂(πN−1)$\hat{p}(\pi _{N-1})$as functions ofp(πNu)$p(\pi _{N}^{u})$,p(πNd)$p(\pi _{N}^{d})$,p̂(πNu)$\hat{p}(\pi _{N}^{u})$, andp̂(πNd)$\hat{p}(\pi _{N}^{d})$defined by (34) and (35). Then att=N−2$t=N-2$, again using (36) and (37), one getsp(πN−2)$p(\pi _{N-2})$andp̂(πN−2)$\hat{p}(\pi _{N-2})$as functions of the equilibrium pricesp(πN−1)$p(\pi _{N-1})$andp̂(πN−1)$\hat{p}(\pi _{N-1})$that obtain for the different possible realizations ofπN−1$\pi _{N-1}$. Iterating, one recovers prices in all states fromt=N−1$t=N-1$tot=1$t=1$. In theAppendix, we prove that there exists a unique pair of prices,(pt(πt),p̂(πt))$(p_{t}(\pi _{t}), \hat{p}(\pi _{t}))$solving (36) and (37). This leads to our next proposition:
  Proposition 5.For any sunspot process such thatπt<π¯$\pi _{t}<\overline{\pi }$for allt≤N$t\le N$, there exists an equilibrium such that:
  
  
  Proposition5shows that, within a given equilibrium, the sunspot(ξt−1,πt)$(\xi _{t-1},\pi _{t})$plays the role of a sequence of coordination variables, generating fluctuations in prices, even when fundamentals (endowmentse, transactional benefits θ) remain constant. These fluctuations correspond to extrinsic volatility.20
  Moreover, there is a large number of possible distributions of the sunspot, each corresponding to a possible equilibrium, so there is a large multiplicity of equilibria. Thus, Proposition5extends the multiplicity results of Proposition3to our new class of equilibria in which prices move in response to sunspots not only when the cryptocurrency crashes but also during arbitrarily many periods before that.
  Proposition 6.Consider a sequence of prices{pt}t=1,..,∞$\lbrace p_{t}\rbrace _{t=1,..,\infty }$satisfying (38). There exists a constantλ>0$\lambda >0$and a sequence of random variablesu∼τ$\tilde{u}_{\tau }$, each with unit expectation (Eτ−1(u∼τ)=1$E_{\tau -1}(\tilde{u}_{\tau })=1$) and independent of the investors' information set in periodτ−1$\tau -1$, such that the new price sequence
  
  Proposition6implies that for any equilibrium price sequence{pt}t=1,..,∞$\lbrace p_{t}\rbrace _{t=1,..,\infty }$, one can construct another equilibrium price sequence{p¯t}t=1,..,∞$\lbrace \bar{p}_{t}\rbrace _{t=1,..,\infty }$. Moreover, when one has gone from{pt}t=1,..,∞$\lbrace p_{t}\rbrace _{t=1,..,\infty }$to{p¯t}t=1,..,∞$\lbrace \bar{p}_{t}\rbrace _{t=1,..,\infty }$, one can iterate the process and go from{p¯t}t=1,..,∞$\lbrace \bar{p}_{t}\rbrace _{t=1,..,\infty }$to yet another equilibrium. Iterating, one can generate a continuum of equilibria. Hence, as in the risk-averse case, there exist multiple sunspot-driven equilibria. Also as in the risk-averse case, within each equilibrium there is extrinsic (sunspot) volatility, so that prices can vary even when fundamentals remain constant. In Proposition6, this extrinsic volatility is the volatility of the random variablesu∼t,t=1,..,∞$\tilde{u }_{t},t=1,..,\infty$.
  To see, intuitively, how Proposition6obtains, take the three following steps. First multiply the left-hand side of (38) byλ(∏τ=1tuτ)$\lambda (\prod _{\tau =1}^{t}u_{\tau })$, which yieldsp¯t$\bar{p}_{t}$. Second, also multiply the right-hand side of (38) byλ(∏τ=1tuτ)$\lambda (\prod _{\tau =1}^{t}u_{\tau })$, so that it is equal top¯t$\bar{p}_{t}$. Third, multiply the terms inside the brackets in (38) byu∼t+1$\tilde{ u}_{t+1}$and note that this does not change the value of the expectation, sinceEt(u∼t+1)=1$E_{t}(\tilde{u}_{t+1})=1$andu∼t+1$\tilde{u}_{t+1}$is independent of the variables in investors' periodtinformation set.
  Proposition 7.With linear utility and strictly concave transactional benefits, if
  
  Thus, in both the risk-averse and the risk-neutral cases, and also for both linear and concave transactional benefits, our theoretical analysis implies that in contrast to perfect-markets equilibrium stock prices, which should not be more volatile than fundamentals (Shiller (1981)), equilibrium currency prices can exhibit sunspot-driven extrinsic volatility that is unrelated to fundamentals.21While stock prices are anchored by real variables such as firm profits, which in perfect markets are not determined by stock prices, currencies have no such real anchors since their fundamental value depends directly on their future price. The lack of real anchor widens the scope for extrinsic volatility.
article.III. Data:
Our theoretical model yields implications about the relationship between cryptocurrency prices, transactional benefits and costs, and crash probabilities. To confront these theoretical implications to the data, we collect data on returns, transactional benefits, and transaction costs.
  Our data set starts on July 17, 2010, with the opening of the MtGox Bitcoin marketplace, and ends on December 31, 2018. Computing a Bitcoin price series over a period of almost nine years is subject to several caveats: new marketplaces, sometimes short-lived, have been created and shut down at a rather high pace, price volatility is high, and there is large price dispersion between exchanges even when trading volumes are high (see Makarov and Schoar (2020)). To construct a time series of Bitcoin prices, we rely on tick-level historical trade data provided byhttp://www.kaiko.com/Kaiko.22We use all transaction prices denominated in five currencies from 20 major exchanges.23Following Paine and Knottenbelt (2016), pooling all transaction prices in each currency, we split each UTC day in 5-minute intervals. In each interval, we compute the volume weighted median price. To construct a daily price for each currency, we then compute an arithmetic (unweighted) average of these median prices. Using medians reduces the effect of outliers. Using weighted medians prevents small trades from having too much influence. Finally, nonweighted averages give equal weight to the information flowing at different times during a day. To obtain a single daily price series, we convert daily prices in each currency to U.S. dollars using daily USD exchange rates from FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data) and compute an unweighted average daily price. This time series is illustrated in Figure4.
  We retrieve Bitcoin transaction fees paid to miners (hereafter referred to as miners' fees) from blockchain data using Blocksci, an open-source software platform for blockchain analysis (Kalodner et al. (2017)). Then, to compute miners' fees in percentage we divide fees by transaction volume. Transaction volume is more difficult to measure (see, for instance, Meiklejohn et al. (2013) or Kalodner et al. (2017)). Because some of the transfers occur among addresses that belong to the same participant, in a pseudonymous network like Bitcoin the identity of the participant corresponding to an address cannot be observed. To estimate Bitcoin transaction volume, we first retrieve the on-chain transaction volume excluding coinbase transactions, that is, transactions that reward miners by the creation of new Bitcoins, and transfers from an address to itself.24From that value, we further exclude amounts that are likely to result from “self churn” behavior, that is, transfers among addresses that belong to the same participant.25The time series of transaction volume is illustrated in Figure5.26
  The time series of miners' fees (in percentage of transaction volume) is depicted in Figure6. The figure illustrates that, during most of the sample period, miners' fees are low. Daily fees amount to 0.0106% of transaction volume, on average. Q1, median, and Q3 are 0.0038%, 0.0057%, and 0.0099%, respectively. There are a few spikes, however. The largest occurs toward the end of 2017, a time when transaction fees exceeded 0.23% due to the congestion triggered by the surge in trading volume (see Easley, O'Hara and Basu (2019), Huberman, Leshno and Moalleni (2021), or Iyidogan (2020) for models of blockchain miners' fees).
  Browsing the web (in particular, Bitcointalk.org), we collect information about all hacks and other losses on Bitcoin. We identify and collect data on about 53 such events over our sample period.27For each event we collect the loss and the time at which it was reported. To obtain percentage losses (to fit our definition ofh), we divide loss amounts byXt$X_{t}$. The resulting time series is shown in Figure7. The corresponding events are listed in TableIA.Iin theInternet Appendix. Most loss events correspond to hacks of exchange platforms, during which private keys that secure access to Bitcoin deposited by platform's clients are stolen by attackers. Besides being hacked, exchange platforms may also lose their private keys (Bitomat, July 2011) or accidentally transfer Bitcoin to incorrect Bitcoin addresses (MtGox, October 2011). The largest loss to date was due to the collapse of MtGox in February 2014, when 744,408 Bitcoin were lost. On average, during the entire sample period, the fraction of Bitcoin lost per week is approximately 0.04%.
  We construct two indicesMarketAccessandBenefitto capture the cumulative impact of these events. To constructMarketAccess, we identify 43 events over our sample period (see TableIA.IIin theInternet Appendix). We consider three categories of events. The first category relates to exchange platforms.28It includes the creation of the first exchange platform on which a given currency can be traded against Bitcoin, or the closure of the last exchange platform on which that currency can be traded. For example, in the case of the Chinese yuan, the first exchange opened on June 13, 2011, while the last closed on September 30, 2017.29The first category of events also includes events associated with the evolutions of these platforms, for example, technological improvements in their payment system (e.g., MtGox eased fund transfers on October 25, 2010) or trading disruptions. The second category relates to regulatory changes that facilitate or impair the trading of Bitcoin, for example the ban of Bitcoin trading by citizens in China as of January 16, 2018. The third category includes miscellaneous but important events, for example, the opening of the first Bitcoin ATM on October 29, 2013 or the start of Bitcoin futures trading at the CBOE on December 10, 2017. Positive events are coded by +1 and negative events by −1. To account for the importance of these events we weight them by the GDP of the country in which they take place, relative to world GDP.30TheMarketAccessindex is the sum of these weighted events: at each point in time, it indicates how easy it is to buy or sell Bitcoins.
  To constructBenefit, we identify 39 events listed in TableIA.IIIin theInternet Appendix. These events fall into two categories. The first category includes new goods and services available for electronic purchase with Bitcoins (e.g., computer hardware, travel agency services, or illegal products). For example, on June 11, 2014, Expedia started accepting Bitcoin for hotel reservations. An example of illegal activity is the opening of SilkRoad on January 23, 2011. The second category corresponds to new payment facilities (gift cards or payment systems that accept Bitcoin). For instance, Paypal started accepting Bitcoin on January 22, 2015. As above, positive events are coded by +1 and negative events are coded by −1. We do not weight these events because it is hard to define an appropriate weighting scheme. TheBenefitindex is the sum of these events: at each timet, it indicates the extent of goods and services that can be purchased with Bitcoins.
  The time series of the two indices is provided in Figure8. TheMarketAccessindex increased sharply over the first two years, as new exchange platforms allowing trades between Bitcoin and new currencies opened. Two major events triggered a sharp decrease in the index in 2013: MtGox suspended fund transfers on May 14, 2013 and China banned financial institutions from using Bitcoin on December 3, 2013. TheBenefitindex remained low in the first years of the sample period, reflect the fact that it was hard to use Bitcoin to purchase goods and services. The index started to increase toward the end of 2013 and reached its maximum in 2018. It then decreased somewhat, as some large companies stopped accepting payments in Bitcoin.
article.IV. Calibration:
In this section, we calibrate the model by evaluating the order of magnitude of the coefficients that offer a relatively good match between the model and the data.31
  We perform our calibration under the assumption that investors are risk-neutral. This assumption is likely to be innocuous because, during our sample period (2010 to 2018), the capitalization of Bitcoin was only a small fraction of aggregate wealth, so variations in Bitcoin prices likely did not move marginal consumption much. Indeed, Liu and Tsyvinski (2021) find empirically that the correlation between Bitcoin returns and durable or nondurable consumption growth, industrial production growth and personal income growth is economically and statistically insignificant. Below, we present the model that is calibrated, the proxies for the model variablesh, θ, and ϕ, and the specification of the probability of a crashπt$\pi _{t}$.
  Proposition 8.When investors are risk-neutral and periodt+1$t+1$inflation in the standard currency is known in periodt, the rate of return on the cryptocurrency price expressed in standard currency is such that
  
  Equation (52) states that the return on the cryptocurrency must compensate investors for crashes, transactions costs and hacks, minus transactional benefits.
  To avoid day-of-the-week effects while keeping a reasonable amount of data, the daily price series is downsampled to a weekly frequency. The final sample used in the calibration contains 432 observations and runs from the week of September 26, 2010 through the week of December 23, 2018.35Several forks occurred in our sample period that granted Bitcoin owners additional coins in the newly created currency. These coins can be interpreted as a form of dividend and we therefore add them to thet+1$t+1$Bitcoin price when computing the Bitcoin return fromttot+1$t+1$. TableIA.IVin theInternet Appendixpresents the forks considered and the value of the new currencies.
  The top panel of Figure9plots the raw weekly Bitcoin return series in USD, net of hacked coins. This is the series for which the equilibrium pricing equation (51) should hold. The bottom panel of the figure plots a smoothed version of the return series. The raw weekly Bitcoin return exhibits substantial variation. Its mean is 3.9% with a standard deviation of 17.3%, a minimum of −40.4%, and a maximum of 110.3%. The smoothed series helps visualize a low-frequency trend of a generally declining return over the course of the sample. We revisit this plot after the model has been calibrated, overlaying it with the model-implied required expected return to verify model fit.
  Calibration proceeds as follows. We replace model variables in (51) by their proxies defined above, and we define the error as the difference between the model-implied Bitcoin return in the next period and the realized return. We then perform our calibration by minimizing the root mean squared errors (RMSE).
  We emphasize that the goal of our calibration exercise is simply to examine whether there exist parameters that provide a good match of the model to the data. We stop short of actually estimating population parameters. Such an estimation would be difficult given the statistical problems associated with our short sample size and the likely nonstationarity of our variables. Solving these problems is beyond the scope of the present paper and hence, is left for further research.
  TableIpresents the calibrated parameter values. Consistent with intuition, calibrated Bitcoin required expected returns decrease in the proxy for transactional benefits (α1>0$\alpha _{1}>0$) and increase in the cost of market access (β1>0$\beta _{1}>0$andβ2>0$\beta _{2}>0$). Again, given the above-mentioned statistical problems, we do not claim statistical significance of α1, β1, or β2.
  A natural way to judge model fit is to plot the smoothed realized Bitcoin return of Figure9and overlay it with the calibrated required expected return implied by (51). Figure11shows that our required-return series track the time-varying mean of the realized return series reasonably well.
  To assess economic significance of the variables that drive the required return, it is useful to decompose this total return across these variables. The linearised version of the model in (52) allows us to do so. Figure12thus illustrates the decomposition of the calibrated required expected return. The top graph depicts the total required expected return, which is the sum of the five components that follow in the five graphs below it. We find that the required weekly Bitcoin return starts at a very high level (between 8% and 18%) during the first two years of our sample period, 2010 and 2011. Expected weekly returns remain high, between 2% and 8%, for the next couple of years. Then, expected returns are lower, around 2%, during the rest of the sample period, except toward the end of 2017.
  Figure12also shows that crash risk explains a large fraction of the required expected return (around 11 percentage points) during the first two years of the sample period. As time goes by, however, and no crash is observed, Bayesian updating leads to a decline in the conditional probability of a crash, which converges to zero. Figure12also shows that costs associated with mining fees, delays and congestion on the blockchain are negligible, with the notable exception of 2017. Toward the end of 2017, the costs of mining fees, delays, and congestion contribute to 10 percentage points of Bitcoin required return. Figure12also shows that the index proxying for the difficulty in exchanging Bitcoin for standard currency contributes almost 10 percentage points to the required return at the beginning of the sample, and then around 8 percentage points thereafter. The contribution of hack risk is relatively small, amounting to only 4 basis points.
  Against these costs, Figure12shows that the transactional benefit component (θ) starts around zero at the beginning of the sample but increases until 2015. The calibrated transactional benefit, which underlies the fundamental value of the cryptocurrency, is then around 8%. Such a large magnitude may be viewed as implausible. It is useful, however, to compare this magnitude to that of, for instance, the cost of cross-border fund transfers. According to World Bank data, the cost of remittances is around 6%.37To that amount, for countries with severe capital controls, one must add the cost of avoiding these controls.
  One reason the calibrated parameters are so large could be that realized returns have been consistently higher than expected returns. The wedge between realized and expected returns could reflect the possibility of a rare disaster such as a crash in the Bitcoin price (“peso problem”). We tried to overcome this issue by introducing a probability of a crash driving the Bitcoin price to zero. Since there was no crash, and in the calibration the probability of a crash evolves with Bayesian updating, it becomes very small after two years. With another modeling choice, as suggested in SectionIV.A, the calibrated probability of a crash could remain larger over the entire sample. This could yield smaller and more plausible net transactional benefit parameters. Fully incorporating these elements in the analysis is left for future research.
  Although our calibration quantifies the effect of fundamentals on required expected returns, it also provides information on how much of the time-variation in Bitcoin returns can be attributed to a model-implied required expected returns. To do so, let us compute anR2. The standard deviation of the calibrated model-implied required expected return is 3.9%. The standard deviation of realized returns is much larger, since it is 17.3%. TheR2is therefore(3.9%)2/(17.3%)2=5.2%$(3.9\%)^2/(17.3\%)^2 = 5.2\%$. Thus, changes in fundamental variables explain only a small fraction of the variation in Bitcoin returns.38To interpret the result that fundamentals explain only 5.2% of the variance of Bitcoin returns, it is useful to bear in mind that, in our theoretical model, return volatility can reflect extrinsic noise in addition to changes in fundamental variables, as stated in Proposition6. Thus, in the framework of our model, our empirical results suggest a decomposition of the total variance of Bitcoin returns: 5.2% stems from changes in fundamentals, while the remaining 94.8% reflects extrinsic noise.39
article.V. Conclusion:
We build an overlapping generations rational expectation equilibrium model relating the price of a cryptocurrency to its fundamentals: transaction costs and benefits. The model shows how these fundamentals should be priced, and highlights the interaction between expected future prices and fundamentals. The model also shows that equilibrium price volatility can be increased by extrinsic volatility unrelated to fundamentals.
  We then calibrate the equilibrium pricing equation, relying on a hand-collected data set of fundamental events that affect the ease with which agents can transact in Bitcoins. Using these data, we construct proxies for the Bitcoin fundamentals: its transaction costs and benefits. We show that these fundamentals are significant determinants of Bitcoin returns, and we provide quantitative measures of their relative importance over time. Consistent with our theoretical implication that equilibrium prices can exhibit extrinsic volatility, our calibration also shows that a large part of the variation in prices is not explained by changes in fundamentals.
  Reflecting the very large realized Bitcoin returns, our calibrated transactional costs and benefits are very large, and arguably implausible. This calls for extensions of our framework that could better rationalize observed returns. Our calibration relies on a simple Bayesian specification of agents' beliefs about crash risk. Richer specifications, possibly allowing for differences in beliefs, or keeping the probability of crash high even after a long period without crash, could help match the data with more plausible parameters.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
